description: 100bb deep. BTN (Hero) opens 2.5bb, BB 3-bets to 11bb, Hero calls. Flop Kh 7d 2s. BB checks. Pot 22.5bb. Action on Hero.
framing: BB's range is heavily capped — they'd c-bet most KK/AA/AK for value and protection
BTN has range disadvantage on Kh 7d 2s because BB has more Kx in their 3-bet range
SPR ~4 with 89bb behind — postflop play is range-vs-range, not stack-deep
BTN's cold-call range here is mostly Qx/Jx/TT-77 and backdoor draws; very little Kx
Dry, high-equity flop where the OOP cold-checker has surrendered initiative

pros: BB has more Kx in their 3-bet range — Hero is range-disadvantaged on K72
BB's range is capped — they'd c-bet most KK/AA/AK for protection and value
Realizes equity cheaply with weak pairs, backdoor draws, and gutshots
Avoids getting raised off equity by the rare slow-played Kx
cons: Loses thin value from Qx/Jx that would call a small bet
Lets BB realize equity with overcards on most turns
Wet turns (A, Q, J, 9) make Hero's IP advantage harder to leverage

pros: Folds out air with backdoor equity that would otherwise see a free turn
Charges weak underpairs and overcards a small price to continue
Keeps the pot small if BB check-raises
cons: BB's range is so capped a c-bet mostly folds out hands you already beat
Hero has very little Kx in a 3-bet-flat range, so the polar story is weak
Loses EV vs check-back when BB calls with all the Qx/Jx/TT that beat you

pros: Maximum pressure on BB's bluff-catchers (TT, 99, AQ, AJ)
Sets up a credible stacks-in story by the river when BB calls
Polarizes Hero's range — credible for the rare Kx + nutted draws
cons: Hero doesn't have enough Kx in a 3-bet-call range to credibly polarize
Gets called by every pair+ that beats Hero's air
Bloats the pot in a spot where check-back was already +EV

gto_explanation: When the 3-bettor checks a high-equity board like K72 rainbow into the in-position cold-caller, the BB's range is heavily capped (they would c-bet most of their KK/AA/AK for protection and value). The BTN cold-caller has a range disadvantage on this board because BB has more Kx in their 3-bet range. Solvers strongly favor a high check-back frequency here to protect the BTN's range and to realize equity with weak pairs and backdoor draws.

villain_ranges.label: BB's 3-bet range vs BTN open
villain_ranges.anchor: BB has more Kx in their 3-bet range
villain_ranges.summary: Linear 3-bet range: JJ+, AK, AQs, plus a slice of suited bluffs (A5s, KQs, etc.)

villain_ranges.label: BB's pure value (the protection bets)
villain_ranges.anchor: KK/AA/AK for protection and value
villain_ranges.summary: The hands BB would size up to protect on K72r — overpairs and AK

description: 100bb deep, full ring. Folds to CO. CO opens 2.5bb. BB (Hero) holds A5o. Action on Hero.
framing: CO's open range is wide — roughly the top 25% of hands at 100bb
BB closes the action and gets a discounted price to defend with most hands
A5o is at the very bottom of BB's defending range, not a clean call or fold
Out of position postflop — BB realizes less than 100% of equity on most flops
The ace blocker removes a chunk of CO's AA/AK continuing combos

pros: Avoids playing a dominated offsuit ace out of position
Sidesteps reverse-implied-odds spots when an A flops
Simple line — no postflop guesswork
cons: Concedes too much equity given the price and CO's wide opening range
A5o is one of the canonical 3-bet bluffs — folding gives up that EV
Folds the wheel and ace blocker — both meaningful equity components

pros: Closes the action getting a good price
Sees flops where the wheel or A-x pairs can outflop CO's broadways
Keeps Hero's flatting range from being too capped
cons: A5o realizes equity poorly OOP — dominated postflop by Ax that calls
Wastes the A blocker that would lower CO's 4-bet/call frequency
Lets CO c-bet a wide range against a capped BB flat

pros: Ace blocks AA/AK/AQ — best combos at the top of CO's continue range
Wheel potential makes A5s/A5o legitimate semi-bluff candidates
Recovers fold equity that calling OOP can't capture
Polarizes range correctly — protects flats like KQs/QJs by removing trash
cons: Builds the pot OOP if CO 4-bets light or calls
Gets crushed by the value portion of CO's continue range
Out of position postflop when called, with an unsuited weak ace

gto_explanation: A5o is at the bottom of BB's defending range but is a high-frequency 3-bet bluff candidate, not a flat. The blocker to AA/AK and the wheel-straight potential make it block more value than a hand like K5o, and 3-betting recovers fold equity that calling out of position cannot. Flatting OOP with A5o realizes equity poorly. Solvers 3-bet A5o in BB-vs-CO at high frequency.

description: 100bb. SRP. SB completes, BTN (Hero) raises to 3bb, SB calls. Flop 9c 6c 2h: SB checks, BTN bets 3bb, SB calls. Turn Tc (flush completing). SB checks. Pot 14bb. Action on Hero.
framing: Tc completes the front-door flush draw and is a range-flipping turn card
SB cold-called preflop with all the small suited clubs that flopped a draw and got there
BTN's flop c-bet had many one-pair value hands and few flush combos
Pot 14bb with ~88bb behind — turn sizing dictates the river leverage
SB's check-call line on the flop is consistent with many made flushes now

pros: BTN c-bet flop with a polarized range, so most one-pair hands lose equity on the third club
Avoids being check-raised by the calling range's many made flushes
Keeps SB's bluffs and weak pairs in the pot for a river bluff-catch
Realizes equity on rivers where the board pairs or a blank lands
cons: Lets SB realize equity for free with overcards and one-pair hands
Gives up the betting initiative — turns the river into a guess
Misses thin value from SB's worst made hands that would call a small bet

pros: Charges weak Qx and underpairs that just hit a draw
Denies equity to overcards with backdoor flush
Keeps the bet credible without overcommitting one-pair hands
cons: Half-pot looks like value but BTN's value range mostly got drawn out on
Gets called by every flush and raised by the strongest
Underprices SB's continue threshold — they call wide and put Hero in turn/river trouble

pros: Maximum fold equity vs SB's one-pair hands that brick the flush
Charges draws and any non-flush continuance the full price
cons: BTN's value range mostly can't credibly bet pot here — SB has the flushes
Gets snap-called or raised by every made flush in SB's range
Burns 14bb into a calling range that just range-flipped past Hero

pros: Tells the maximum-pressure story with the nut-flush+Ac-blocker combos
If credible, folds out everything except the made flushes
cons: Hero's range can't actually credibly overbet — too few nut flushes in BTN flop c-bet line
Gets called only by hands that beat Hero — pure value-burning vs SB's flushes
An overbet that runs into a check-raise is a disaster for stack preservation

gto_explanation: The turned third club is a brutal scare card for BTN's range — BTN c-bet flop with a polarized range that includes many one-pair hands but few flush combinations relative to SB, who can have all the small suited connectors that flop a draw and got there. BTN's pure value (sets, two pair) is now drawn out on and BTN's bluffs lose equity against the calling range's many made flushes. Solvers strongly favor checking back most of BTN's range on this turn including most one-pair hands, betting only the nut-flush and bluffs containing the Ac blocker.

villain_ranges.label: BTN's flop polarized c-bet range
villain_ranges.anchor: BTN c-bet flop with a polarized range
villain_ranges.summary: Overpairs, sets, top pair, plus suited bluffs with backdoors — few flushes

villain_ranges.label: SB's calling range (now full of flushes)
villain_ranges.anchor: the calling range's many made flushes
villain_ranges.summary: Many small/medium two-club hands that floated the flop now complete: TcXc, 8c7c, 9c5c, etc.

description: 40bb deep, 6-max. UTG (Hero) holds 22. Action on Hero.
framing: 40bb effective is too shallow for standard 100bb opening charts to apply directly
Small pairs (22-44) rely on set-mining implied odds that collapse at low SPR
UTG opens face the entire table — ranges that continue are tight and dominant
At 40bb, stacking off after flopping a set yields ~40bb, not the ~95bb that 100bb math assumes
22 has no equity advantage vs an opening range — it's a pure implied-odds hand

pros: 40bb is too shallow for 22 to set-mine profitably — implied odds collapse
Avoids a dominated postflop spot when called by overcards or 3-bet light
UTG range needs to be tight at this depth — 22 is firmly below the cutoff
Preserves stack for higher-equity spots in late position
cons: Folds a pair that occasionally flops a set and stacks an overpair
Tight image — doesn't pressure the table
Mathematically painful when you fold and the BTN flops a set with 32o

pros: Smaller open risks less when forced to fold to a 3-bet
Keeps the range balanced if Hero opens this size with the rest of UTG
Pot odds when 3-bet are slightly better for set-mining
cons: Still doesn't fix the core problem — 22 lacks implied odds at 40bb
Smaller opens invite multiway flops where 22 plays even worse
Off-mix sizing if the rest of the UTG range opens 3bb — signals a small-pair pattern

pros: Standard sizing — keeps range balanced with the rest of UTG opens
Bigger initial pot if Hero does flop a set
Discourages the loose flatters that punish small pairs postflop
cons: Risks more chips for a hand that can't profitably continue vs 3-bets at 40bb
Set-mining math still fails — stack-to-pot ratio for stacking off post-set is too low
Forces close fold/call decisions vs 3-bets where 22's equity is small

pros: Cheap entry to see a flop in a hand that wants to hit a set
Encourages multiway pots where set value compounds
Hides hand strength if Hero flops well
cons: Limping UTG is broadly losing — invites isolation raises Hero can't continue against
Caps Hero's UTG range and signals weakness to the table
Even when called multiway, 22 misses 7 out of 8 flops with no plan

gto_explanation: At 40bb effective from UTG in 6-max, small pocket pairs like 22-44 no longer have the set-mining odds they need against the depth-adjusted calling/3-betting ranges. Implied odds shrink because if you flop a set against a 3-bettor at 40bb, you cannot get a full 'set value' stacked off the cooler. The mixed-strategy range tightens at this depth and 22 falls below the cutoff for UTG opens. This is a 'shallow-depth UTG fold' that players who open standard 100bb ranges leak with.

description: 100bb. SB (Hero) raises to 3bb, BB calls. Flop Qh 7s 4d: SB bets 2bb, BB calls. Turn 8s: SB checks, BB checks. River 3h: BB checks. Hero holds KQo. Pot 10bb. Action on Hero.
framing: BB check-called flop, then went check-check on the turn — range is condensed around weak made hands
Turn check by both players caps everyone — strong hands (KQ+, sets) would have bet
River 3h is a brick that changes nothing about hand strengths
Hero's KQ is top pair top kicker on a Qh-high board — clear showdown value
Pot 10bb with ~90bb behind — sizing here is about extracting from worse, not leverage

pros: Locks in showdown with TPTK against a checked-down range
Avoids being check-raised by the rare slow-played two pair or set
Saves stack vs a check-call station who only calls with better
cons: Misses thin value — BB's range is full of Qx with worse kickers (Q9, QT, QJ)
Lets the worst Qx and middle pairs see a free showdown
Underplays one of the strongest one-pair hands at showdown

pros: Extracts thin value from worse Qx and middle pairs that pay one bet
Small size lets Hero balance with cheap bluffs from busted draws
Maintains range advantage on the merged check-call-check line
Folds out almost nothing Hero beats — pure value capture
cons: Can get check-raised on a board where 65/55/44 are credible BB hands
Misses bigger value vs the rare two pair BB slow-played
BB station-types might overcall a bigger size you left on the table

pros: Bigger value if BB does have a sticky Qx or middle pair
Denies equity from the rare backdoor hand BB held to the river
cons: Worse Qx folds at this size — turns thin value into thin air
Builds pot for the bottom of Hero's value range
Bluff-to-value ratio gets ugly without enough bluffs to balance

pros: If BB does have a hidden monster you collect maximum
Sells the bluff perfectly when Hero has nothing
cons: Every Qx with worse kicker folds — Hero's value range can't get paid
Range can't credibly overbet here — no two pair, no nut hands
An overbet folding out the entire value target turns a +EV bet into -EV

gto_explanation: KQ is top pair top kicker with strong showdown value, but on a river where BB checks twice on a low-action runout BB's range is full of Qx with worse kickers (Q9, QT, QJ at frequency), middle pairs, and busted spade draws. A small thin-value bet around 30% pot extracts value from worse Qx and weak middle pairs (8x, 7x) that will call once but never twice, while keeping the bluff range balanced. Over-betting bloats the pot against a range that has very little KQ+ left after the BB check-called flop and checked turn.

villain_ranges.label: BB's check-call-check-check range
villain_ranges.anchor: BB's range is full of Qx with worse kickers
villain_ranges.summary: Mostly weaker Qx (Q9-QJ), middle pairs, and busted spade draws after calling flop and checking twice

description: 100bb. CO opens 2.5bb, BTN (Hero) calls with 99. Flop 8h 8d 3c (paired). CO bets 5bb into 6.5bb. Action on Hero.
framing: Paired flops compress everyone's ranges — fewer hands materially change strength
CO's overbet (~75% pot) is polarizing — value 8x/overpairs and air, thin in the middle
BTN flatted preflop with no 99-specific advantage — the pair-9 is just one combo
On 8h 8d 3c the 8 hits CO's range (any small suited connector / pocket pair)
BTN is in position with 99 — controls the pot on most turn cards

pros: Avoids tough turn/river decisions on a paired board
Sidesteps the risk that CO has an eight or a slow-played overpair
Saves chips if CO's range is unusually value-heavy
cons: Far too tight — 99 beats every bluff and most of CO's middling pairs
Folds the second-best class of overpair on a board where CO's value is rare
Gives up to a polar c-bet exactly when the math says to defend wide

pros: Beats CO's entire bluff range and most of their middling pairs
Keeps CO's polar c-betting range intact for turn barrels Hero can fold to
Pot-controls a hand that doesn't want to play a 100bb pot
Re-evaluates on the turn — many cards make this an easy decision
cons: Vulnerable to an overcard turn (any A/K/Q/J/T) flipping initiative
Caps Hero's range — a turn check-raise won't be very credible
Lets CO barrel a polarized turn with all their bluff candidates

pros: Builds the pot vs CO's middling pairs that might pay off
Denies equity to the few overcards CO has with backdoor draws
Takes the initiative on a board where 99 is near the top of Hero's range
cons: Folds out CO's polar c-betting range — the bluffs you were beating
Gets called by AA/KK/QQ/JJ/TT that have 99 in awful shape
Bloats the pot with a one-pair hand on a paired board where 8x lurks

gto_explanation: 99 is an overpair to the board but the texture is dynamic against CO's polar c-betting range. Raising blows out the small pairs and weak overcards CO is bluff-c-betting, which is the part of CO's range we beat. Folding is far too tight — we beat all bluffs and many of CO's middling pairs. Calling keeps CO's bluffs in and lets us reassess on the turn against a polarized barreling range. Solvers mostly call 99 here.
lesson_tag: Call don't raise vs polar c-bet

villain_ranges.label: CO's polar c-bet range on 8-8-3
villain_ranges.anchor: CO's polar c-betting range
villain_ranges.summary: Overpairs (TT+), some Ax value, plus suited connector/broadway bluffs

villain_ranges.label: CO's middling pairs (the thin-value slice)
villain_ranges.anchor: CO's middling pairs
villain_ranges.summary: TT-77 — overpairs to most of the board that c-bet small for protection

description: 100bb. UTG opens 2.5bb, BTN (Hero) calls. Flop Tc 9c 5d: UTG bets 4bb, BTN calls. Turn 2h: UTG bets 11bb, BTN calls. River 6s: UTG bets 50bb (overbet). Hero holds JTs. Pot was 39bb. Action on Hero.
framing: UTG triple-barreled — sizing escalated each street into an overbet on a dry river
Final pot is ~89bb after the overbet; required equity to call is just over 30%
JTs missed every draw (no flush, no straight) and has only top pair decent kicker
JT blocks no straights — both 87 and 8-7 suited combos remain in UTG's value range
UTG's overbet line condenses to sets, two pair, JJ+, and missed flush-draw bluffs

pros: UTG's triple-barrel overbet line on a relatively dry river polarizes to sets, two pair, JJ+, and rivered straights
JT blocks zero of UTG's value combos — no straight blocker, no flush blocker
MDF against an overbet is low — folding marginal bluff-catchers is correct
Avoids burning 50bb to catch a sliver of missed-draw bluffs
cons: Gives up to a single big bet without seeing the showdown
If UTG ever overbluffs this line, folding loses guaranteed equity
JT does beat the missed flush draws that make up the bluff portion

pros: JT is top pair decent kicker — beats every bluff combo UTG could have
Pot odds say defend roughly one-third — JT is plausibly in that pile
Catches all the missed clubs/gutshots that turned hopeless on the river
cons: UTG's value range is enormous on this runout — every combo of 87, 88, 99, sets
JT blocks none of those value combos, so the bluff-catch ratio is awful
An overbet sizing demands strong blockers — JT has none
Burns 50bb on a hand that's structurally a sell vs this line

gto_explanation: TJ on this runout is a marginal bluff-catcher with top pair decent kicker, but UTG's triple-barrel overbet line on a relatively dry river polarizes to sets, two pair, JJ+, and rivered straights (87, 8c7c missing some), with bluffs primarily missed flush draws. The minimum-defense frequency against an overbet of this size demands continuing only the strongest bluff-catchers; TJ blocks none of UTG's value combos (no straight blocker, no flush blocker since no club). Solvers fold TJ at high frequency in this exact spot.
lesson_tag: MDF and blockers vs polar river overbet

villain_ranges.label: UTG's triple-barrel overbet range
villain_ranges.anchor: UTG's triple-barrel overbet line on a relatively dry river polarizes to sets, two pair, JJ+, and rivered straights
villain_ranges.summary: Polar: sets (TT/99/55), JJ+, two pair, rivered straights (87), and missed flush-draw bluffs

description: 100bb. CO (Hero) opens 2.5bb with 55. BTN 3-bets to 9bb. Action on Hero.
framing: 100bb deep — set-mining math works when implied odds can pay ~8x the call
CO is in position vs BTN's 3-bet (closes flop action with positional advantage)
55 flops a set ~1 in 8.5 — needs to win ~7.5x the call when it hits
BTN's 3-bet IP range is tight and value-leaning, full of overpair hands
Pot odds: paying 6.5bb to win 12.5bb immediately, with deep implied behind

pros: Avoids playing a small pair OOP in a bloated 3-bet pot
Sidesteps reverse-implied-odds when 55 flops 66+
Simple line — no postflop guesswork
cons: Folds clean set-mining odds — 100bb deep, paying 6.5 to win ~200 when set hits
Gives up the rare cooler over the 3-betting continue range
Too tight in position with implied odds in hand

pros: Set-mining odds are clean at 100bb — 1-in-8.5 sets pay big when they hit
In position postflop — controls the pot on whiff flops
Cooler equity vs the 3-betting continue range when 55 turns into a set
Closes the action with a defined plan: hit set or fold
cons: Misses the set 88% of the time and check-folds the flop
Vulnerable to squeeze if blinds wake up behind
Even when set hits, board can come with overs that scare villain's range away

pros: Picks up dead money if BTN folds the bottom of their 3-bet range
Disguises Hero's value 4-bet range if balanced with monsters
cons: 55 has poor equity vs BTN's 4-bet-call range (QQ+, AK)
Folds out the bluffs Hero was beating with set value
Turns a hand with implied odds into a face-up bluff
Must fold to a 5-bet shove, surrendering all the set-mine equity

gto_explanation: 55 has clean set-mining odds against a 3-bet to 9bb when 100bb deep: paying 6.5bb to win a pot that can become ~200bb when you flop a set roughly 1 in 8.5 times gives the implied odds needed. 4-betting bloats the pot with a hand that has poor equity against the 3-betting continue range and that performs poorly post-flop without a set. Folding is too tight in position with set-mining odds and a hand that can still cooler aces.

villain_ranges.label: BTN's 3-bet continue range
villain_ranges.anchor: the 3-betting continue range
villain_ranges.summary: BTN's 3-bet vs CO open: TT+, AQ+, plus suited broadways and a few suited bluffs

description: 100bb. HJ opens 2.5bb, BB (Hero) calls with KJs. Flop Kc 5h 4s: BB checks, HJ bets 3bb, BB calls. Turn 7c: BB checks, HJ bets 9bb. Action on Hero.
framing: HJ has the preflop initiative and BB is OOP on every street
Kc 5h 4s flop hits HJ's range hard — overpairs, AK, KQ all continuation-bet
7c turn adds a backdoor flush draw and connects with some middling pairs
KJs is top pair good kicker — strong showdown value vs HJ's range
Sizing ~70% pot on the turn — HJ's range is polar between Kx+ value and missed bluffs

pros: Avoids tough river decisions OOP with one pair
Saves chips if HJ never bluffs the turn
Sidesteps being check-raised off equity
cons: Far too tight — we beat all of HJ's bluffs and many of their thinner value bets (KT, K9 in some constructions, JJ-99)
Folds top pair decent kicker to a standard double-barrel size
Gives up the line opponents are designed to defend with

pros: Beats every bluff and most thin value HJ can barrel
Realizes equity through the river with a clearly-ahead hand
Keeps HJ's bluffs in for a river bluff-catch or value bet
Pot odds say defend — KJ is exactly the hand the line is built for
cons: OOP on the river — facing a third barrel becomes painful
Some turn cards (Q, T, 9) demote KJ from clear ahead to flipping
Caps Hero's range — turn check-raises later in the line look bluff-heavy

pros: Builds the pot vs HJ's barrel range when ahead
Folds out HJ's overcard bluffs immediately
cons: Turns KJ into a bluff — removes the bluffs Hero currently beats
Gets it in vs sets, AK, and the part of HJ's range that has KJ crushed
Burns equity by polarizing a hand that wants to stay merged
Vulnerable to a 3-bet shove that forces a fold of the best hand

gto_explanation: KJ is top pair with a decent kicker. Folding to a turn double barrel sized at ~70% pot when we beat all of HJ's bluffs and many of their thinner value bets (KT, K9 in some constructions, JJ-99) is far too tight. Check-raising turns our hand into a bluff and removes bluffs we beat from HJ's range. Solvers call KJ at very high frequency on K54r-7 here — it is precisely the type of hand the line is designed to defend.

villain_ranges.label: HJ's double-barrel range on K54r-7
villain_ranges.anchor: we beat all of HJ's bluffs and many of their thinner value bets (KT, K9 in some constructions, JJ-99)
villain_ranges.summary: Sets, AK, KQ for value plus bluffs with backdoors (QJs, JTs, 65s, A-high with bdfd)

description: 100bb. CO opens 2.5bb, BB (Hero) calls with 8h 4h. Flop 7c 6c 5d. Pot 5.5bb. Action on Hero.
framing: Low connected boards (765, 654) are the canonical donk-lead textures for BB
BB has all the small suited connectors (87s, 76s, 65s, 54s) that CO opens don't include
CO's preflop range is broadway-heavy — many overcards that brick this flop
84o has open-ended draw potential and blocks one of the two key straight cards (87)
Pot 5.5bb — a small donk sizing leverages range advantage without committing

pros: Standard line — keeps the donk range simple by not building one
Avoids being raised off equity by CO's overpairs
Lets CO c-bet and lose with their air
cons: Surrenders BB's range advantage on 765 — Hero has all the small connectors
Misses the small EV gain from donking with nutted+equity combos
Gives CO a free check-back option that they would take with overcards

pros: BB has range advantage on connected low boards — leads charge that edge
84-type combos have clean wheel equity, block 87, and barrel well
Folds out CO's overcards that would otherwise see a free turn
Sets up profitable turn barrels on cards that hit Hero's perceived range
cons: Off-meta line — many opponents won't fold enough to a donk
Gets raised by overpairs that don't believe BB's lead range
Telegraphs a polarized range to observant opponents
Requires balanced construction or it becomes face-up

pros: Bigger fold equity vs CO's overcards and overpairs
If Hero turns the straight, the bigger pot pays off massively
cons: Polarizes Hero's range too hard — 70% donks should be nutted
Gets called by overpairs that 35% would have folded a turn barrel into
Burns chips on a draw without commensurate fold equity vs CO's strong range
Single bad turn (overcard) leaves Hero face-up

gto_explanation: On low connected boards like 765 the BB has a range advantage relative to the CO opener because BB calls all the small connectors (87, 76s, 65s, 54s, 87s for some constructions) that the CO does not open. Solvers have begun donking these boards at meaningful frequencies with the nutted part of the range (made straights) plus bluffs that have wheel equity. 84o is a strong donking bluff candidate here — it has clean equity to backdoor straight, blocks 87, and turns into a barrel target. Solvers donk lead BB on 765-type boards at ~25-40% frequency.

description: 12bb deep, MTT bubble. Folds to BTN (Hero) with A8o. Action on Hero.
framing: 12bb effective is squarely in push-fold territory — Nash charts replace standard ranges
Min-raise/fold is structurally broken at this depth — too small to fold to a 3-bet shove
MTT bubble dynamics matter but Hero is on BTN attacking only two blinds
A8o has live cards and decent equity vs any non-pair calling range
The blinds need a tight calling range to call a 12bb shove — fold equity is real

pros: Avoids being called by AA-99/AT+ when behind
Saves 12bb for a better spot
Tight image preserved for future steal attempts
cons: Massive Nash leak — A8o from BTN at 12bb is a clear shove
Folds equity vs the wide range that can't continue without dominating A8
Bleeds the stack via blinds that the shove was meant to attack
Lets blinds realize equity for free with hands they'd fold to a shove

pros: Forces the blinds to call only with hands that dominate A8 or coin-flip with it
Picks up dead money immediately when both blinds fold
Realizes A8's equity through showdown when called by worse
Avoids the awful min-raise-then-face-shove decision at this depth
cons: Called by exactly the hands that crush A8 (AK, AQ, AT, AJ, TT+)
12bb is enough for callers to make a clear value call with marginal hands
Variance can sting — losing the flip ends the tournament
Reveals stack depth and aggression pattern

pros: Smaller commitment if the blinds 3-bet shove and Hero must fold
Could induce a fold from the absolute bottom of blinds' range
Keeps more chips alive when Hero gets it in unimproved
cons: Terrible at this depth — can't fold to a 3-bet shove, can't profitably call
Lets blinds realize equity cheaply when they flat
Loses the fold-equity edge a shove provides
Worst of both worlds — risks too much to fold, too little to deny equity

gto_explanation: At 12bb effective from the BTN A8 offsuit is a clear push in Nash open-shove tables. Min-raising at this depth creates terrible play decisions — you can't fold to a 3-bet shove but you put yourself in marginal flips for half your stack, and you let the blinds realize their equity. Shoving denies that equity and forces the blinds to call only with hands that dominate A8 or coin-flip with it. Folding A8o at 12bb on the BTN is a major Nash leak.

villain_ranges.label: Blinds' calling range vs BTN 12bb shove
villain_ranges.anchor: forces the blinds to call only with hands that dominate A8 or coin-flip with it
villain_ranges.summary: Roughly 22+, A9+, KJ+, QJs — value hands that beat A8o or flip with it
